questions.q: Which animal's head does the deity Ganesha bear in Hindu tradition?
questions.explanation: Ganesha is famously depicted with the head of an elephant, restored to him after his original head was severed.

questions.q: Ganesha is widely revered as the remover of obstacles. By which epithet is this role known?
questions.explanation: Ganesha is invoked as Vighnaharta, the remover of obstacles, before the start of new undertakings.

questions.q: Who are the divine parents of Ganesha according to the Puranas?
questions.explanation: The Puranas describe Ganesha as the son of Shiva and Parvati.

questions.q: Which small creature serves as the vahana (mount) of Ganesha?
questions.explanation: Ganesha's vahana is a mouse, often named Mushika, on which he is depicted riding.

questions.q: Ganesha is the patron deity invoked at the start of what kinds of activities?
questions.explanation: As the lord of beginnings, Ganesha is invoked before new ventures and undertakings to ensure success.

questions.q: Which epithet of Ganesha refers to his single broken tusk?
questions.explanation: Ganesha is called Ekadanta, meaning 'one-tusked', in reference to his broken tusk.

questions.q: Which warrior god is traditionally regarded as the brother of Ganesha?
questions.explanation: Kartikeya, also called Skanda or Murugan, is the brother of Ganesha, both being sons of Shiva and Parvati.

questions.q: According to the Puranas, Ganesha served as the scribe for which great Sanskrit epic?
questions.explanation: The Puranic tradition relates that Ganesha wrote down the Mahabharata as the sage Vyasa dictated it.

questions.q: Ganesha is honored as the patron of which intellectual domain?
questions.explanation: Ganesha is revered as the patron of letters, wisdom, and learning.

questions.q: Which sweet is traditionally regarded as a favourite offering to Ganesha?
questions.explanation: The sweet dumpling called modak is the offering most associated with Ganesha in devotional tradition.

questions.q: According to the Puranas, why did Shiva behead the boy who would become Ganesha?
options: The boy guarded Parvati's door and barred Shiva's entry
questions.explanation: The Puranas relate that the boy, set by Parvati to guard her door, refused Shiva entry, and Shiva in anger severed his head.

questions.q: After beheading the boy, with what did Shiva restore him to life as Ganesha?
options: A golden head fashioned by Vishvakarma
The head of the first creature found, an elephant
questions.explanation: Shiva restored the boy by fixing upon him the head of an elephant, the first creature his attendants encountered.

questions.q: Which implement, said to draw devotees toward truth, is commonly held in one of Ganesha's hands?
questions.explanation: Ganesha is often depicted holding a noose (pasha), symbolising the capture of obstacles and worldly attachments.

questions.q: Besides the noose, which goad-like implement is frequently shown in Ganesha's hand?
questions.explanation: Ganesha commonly holds an ankusha, the elephant goad, used to prod the mind forward and remove hindrances.

questions.q: By which name, meaning 'lord of the ganas', is Ganesha widely known?
questions.explanation: Ganesha is called Ganapati, meaning the lord of the ganas, the attendant hosts of Shiva.

questions.q: In one well-known tale, Ganesha won a contest by circling whom as his entire world?
questions.explanation: Ganesha won the contest by circumambulating his parents Shiva and Parvati, declaring them to be his whole world.

questions.q: Whom did Ganesha race in the circumambulation contest that he won through devotion rather than speed?
questions.explanation: Ganesha's brother Kartikeya raced around the world on his peacock, while Ganesha won by circling their parents.

questions.q: What was the prize at stake in the famous contest between Ganesha and his brother Kartikeya?
questions.explanation: In the popular Puranic tale, the brothers competed for a fruit, and Ganesha won it through his clever devotion to his parents.

questions.q: During the festival devoted to Ganesha, clay images of him are traditionally concluded with which act?
questions.explanation: The festival of Ganesh Chaturthi concludes with visarjan, the immersion of Ganesha's clay image in water.

questions.q: Which festival is celebrated each year as the birth of Ganesha?
questions.explanation: Ganesh Chaturthi is the festival observed in honour of the birth of Ganesha.

questions.q: Ganesha's large ears in his iconography are commonly understood to symbolise what virtue?
questions.explanation: Ganesha's broad ears are traditionally read as a symbol of attentive listening and the gaining of wisdom.

questions.q: Which goddess of prosperity is often depicted and worshipped alongside Ganesha, especially during Diwali?
questions.explanation: Ganesha is frequently worshipped together with Lakshmi, the goddess of wealth and prosperity, particularly at Diwali.

questions.q: In one Puranic account, Ganesha broke off his own tusk to use it as an instrument for what purpose?
options: To write down the Mahabharata when his pen failed
questions.explanation: One tradition holds that Ganesha snapped off his tusk to continue writing the Mahabharata when his writing implement broke.

questions.q: According to a Puranic tale, which celestial body laughed at Ganesha and was consequently cursed by him?
questions.explanation: The Puranas relate that the moon mocked Ganesha, who cursed it, which is linked to the custom of not gazing at the moon on Ganesh Chaturthi.

questions.q: Which name, referring to his elephant face, is among the traditional epithets of Ganesha?
questions.explanation: Ganesha is called Gajanana, meaning 'one with the face of an elephant'.

questions.q: In the Ganapati Atharvashirsha, Ganesha is exalted with which sacred syllable as his essence?
questions.explanation: The Ganapati Atharvashirsha identifies Ganesha with the sacred syllable Om, the primordial sound.

questions.q: Whom did Parvati create to guard her door, according to the Puranic account of Ganesha's origin?
options: A boy formed from the turmeric or sandal paste of her body
A golden statue gifted by Vishnu
questions.explanation: The Puranas relate that Parvati fashioned the boy from the paste of her own body and breathed life into him to guard her door.

questions.q: Which detail is commonly shown encircling the belly of Ganesha as a girdle in his iconography?
options: A serpent worn as a belt
questions.explanation: Ganesha is often depicted with a serpent tied around his ample belly, a detail noted in devotional descriptions and the Ganapati Atharvashirsha.

questions.q: Ganesha's mouse mount, Mushika, in some Puranic tales was originally what kind of being?
options: A demon born of the ocean
A gandharva or celestial transformed by a curse
questions.explanation: Several Puranic tales describe the mouse Mushika as a celestial being, such as a cursed gandharva, who became Ganesha's vahana.

questions.q: In the Ganesha Purana and related texts, Ganesha is at times described battling and subduing what kind of adversaries?
options: The guardians of the four directions
Sea monsters of the cosmic ocean
questions.explanation: The Ganesha Purana recounts Ganesha defeating asuras and demons that personify obstacles and vices, affirming his role as Vighnaharta.
